scenario_title: Delete an RRset and then add an RR at the same name in one UPDATE

group_1.behavior: Processes the UPDATE sequentially, so the delete removes the original A RRset and the later add recreates it.

group_2.behavior: Treats the same-message delete/add combination as conflicting or resolves it differently, so the add does not produce the same final RRset state.

expected_divergence.why_divergence: RFC 2136 §3.4.2 and §3.4.2.7 define the UPDATE operation classes and delete_rrset semantics, but do not specify an ordering rule when a single UPDATE message deletes an RRset and then adds an RR at the same owner name/type. RFC 2181 §5.2 describes RRset consistency, yet it does not resolve same-message conflict handling. As a result, implementations may apply operations sequentially or may treat the combination as inconsistent, producing different final states and potentially different replies.
expected_divergence.test_rationale: The test seeds one A RRset at flip.campus.edu., then sends a single UPDATE that deletes that RRset and immediately adds a new A RR at the same name. If the server processes the operations sequentially, the query afterward should return the new A RRset; if it resolves the message differently, the RRset may be absent or otherwise not match the added data. This directly probes the underspecified intra-message ordering semantics in RFC 2136.
